title: Trait conformance: refinement chains
analysis: The public `check_trait_conformance()` (from task 182) checks a single `CompiledTrait` against a structure's members — its doc explicitly states 'Refinement hierarchy walking remains the caller's responsibility.' The private `collect_all_requirements()` already walks refinement chains recursively with dedup/conflict detection, but it's tightly coupled to `check_and_apply_trait_conformance()` which produces `Diagnostic`s and injects defaults. Task 186 creates a new public function `check_trait_conformance_chain()` that walks refinement chains and returns structured `Vec<ConformanceError>` — bridging the pure validation API (task 182) with chain-aware semantics. Two new `ConformanceError` variants are needed: `ConflictingRequirement` (same member name, different types across chain) and `UnresolvedTrait` (refinement references unknown trait). The function reuses `check_trait_conformance` for per-requirement checking after collecting/deduplicating requirements from the chain.

steps.description: Write failing test `chain_single_trait_no_refinement`: call `check_trait_conformance_chain` on a trait with no refinements and a structure missing a param → expect MissingParam. Fails because function does not exist yet.

Add `check_trait_conformance_chain(structure_members, trait_name, trait_registry, ports, subs) -> Vec<ConformanceError>` to lib.rs. For the base case: look up trait_name in registry, delegate to existing `check_trait_conformance`. Test passes.

Write failing test `chain_two_level_walks_parent`: trait B requires `width : Length`, trait A refines B and requires `height : Length`. Call chain fn on trait A; structure has `height` but not `width` → MissingParam for `width`. Fails because chain walking not implemented.

Implement recursive refinement walking in `check_trait_conformance_chain`: before checking the current trait, recurse into each refinement. Collect all requirements into a Vec with a `visited: HashSet<String>` and `seen_names: HashMap<String, RequirementKind>` for dedup. Check all collected requirements against structure members. Test passes.
Write test `chain_three_level_all_satisfied`: C requires `x`, B : C requires `y`, A : B requires `z`. Structure provides all three → no errors. Verifies multi-level recursive walking works.
Verify test passes with existing recursive implementation from step-4. No code changes expected — commit confirms multi-level chains work.
Write test `chain_missing_deep_ancestor_member`: A : B : C, C requires `base : Length`, structure provides A's and B's members but not `base` → exactly 1 MissingParam error for `base`.
Verify test passes with existing recursive implementation. No code changes expected — commit confirms deep ancestor error propagation.

Write test `chain_diamond_dedup_satisfied`: C requires `x : Length`. A : C, B : C. Call chain fn with both A and B via `check_trait_conformance_multi` helper (simulating `structure : A + B`). Structure has `x` → no errors, requirement from C not checked twice.

Add `check_trait_conformance_multi(structure_members, trait_names, trait_registry, ports, subs) -> Vec<ConformanceError>` that iterates over multiple trait bounds sharing a visited set, preventing duplicate chain walks. Test passes.
Write test `chain_diamond_missing_exactly_one_error`: same diamond (A : C, B : C, C requires `x`), but structure lacks `x` → exactly 1 MissingParam error (not 2 from walking C via both A and B).
Dedup in visited set ensures C's requirement added only once. Verify test passes — no additional code changes expected.

Write failing test `chain_conflicting_requirements`: C requires `x : Length`, D requires `x : Mass`. Trait A refines both C and D. Call chain fn → expect ConflictingRequirement { name: 'x', type_a: Length, type_b: Mass }. Fails because variant does not exist.

Add `ConflictingRequirement { name: String, type_a: Type, type_b: Type }` variant to ConformanceError. In chain walker, when collecting requirements, detect same name with different type via `seen_names` map and emit ConflictingRequirement. Test passes.

Write failing test `chain_unresolved_trait`: trait A has refinement 'Nonexistent' (not in registry). Call chain fn → expect UnresolvedTrait { name: 'Nonexistent' }. Fails because variant does not exist.
Add `UnresolvedTrait { name: String }` variant to ConformanceError. In chain walker, when trait_name is not found in trait_registry, push UnresolvedTrait error and return early. Test passes.

Write integration test `integration_deep_chain_defaults_no_duplicate_injection`: C has default `size : Length = 10mm`, A : C, B : C. `structure def X : A + B {}` → compile_first_template, expect exactly 1 'size' value cell (not 2). Verifies no duplicate default injection from shared ancestors in the full compilation pipeline.

Verify existing `collect_all_requirements` dedup logic handles diamond defaults correctly. Test should pass based on existing visited-set logic. If not, fix the dedup in collect_all_requirements. Commit confirms end-to-end chain default injection is correct.

design_decisions.rationale: The existing function has a clean single-trait contract (documented: 'Refinement hierarchy walking remains the caller's responsibility'). Adding registry/chain parameters would break existing callers and conflate two responsibilities. A separate chain-aware function composes with the single-trait checker.

design_decisions.decision: Also add `check_trait_conformance_multi` for multiple trait bounds with shared dedup state
design_decisions.rationale: Diamond patterns (structure : A + B where A : C and B : C) require shared visited/seen state across multiple top-level trait bounds. A multi-trait entry point shares dedup state, preventing duplicate requirement collection from shared ancestors.

design_decisions.decision: Return `Vec<ConformanceError>` (same return type as single-trait checker) with new variants for chain-specific errors
design_decisions.rationale: Keeps the error type unified. Callers can handle all conformance errors with one match. ConflictingRequirement and UnresolvedTrait are legitimate conformance errors that only arise in chain contexts.
Chain walking requires looking up parent traits by name, which needs the registry. The entry point is a trait name, and the function resolves it internally.

design_decisions.decision: Internal chain walker collects all requirements first, then checks — rather than checking each trait independently
design_decisions.rationale: Collect-then-check enables deduplication of requirements from diamond patterns. Checking independently would produce duplicate errors for shared ancestors.

design_decisions.decision: Keep `collect_all_requirements` private; new public function has its own chain-walking logic
design_decisions.rationale: collect_all_requirements is tightly coupled to the internal Diagnostic/default-injection pipeline. The new public function has its own simpler chain-walking logic that returns ConformanceError variants, avoiding coupling to the internal compilation flow.

reuse.how: After collecting all requirements from the chain into a temporary CompiledTrait (or inline Vec), delegate to this function for the actual member-by-member checking against structure members, ports, and subs.

The new chain walker follows the same algorithmic pattern: visited set for diamond dedup, seen_names map for conflict detection, depth-first parent-first recursion. Adapted to emit ConformanceError instead of Diagnostic.
Reuse all four helpers. Unit tests construct CompiledTraits directly (using empty_trait + modifications). Integration tests use compile_first_template to exercise the full pipeline.
Chain function returns the same error variants for per-member failures. Only adds ConflictingRequirement and UnresolvedTrait as new chain-specific variants.
Same pattern: `visited.insert(trait_name)` for cycle/diamond prevention, `seen_names.get()` for requirement conflict detection.
